challenges.title: Person Class
challenges.description:
Create a class called `Person` with an `__init__` method that accepts `name` and `age` parameters and stores them as instance attributes.

Add a method called `greet()` that returns the string:
"Hi, I'm {name} and I'm {age} years old."
challenges.test_code:
p1 = Person("Alice", 30)
assert p1.name == "Alice", f"name should be 'Alice', got '{p1.name}'"
assert p1.age == 30, f"age should be 30, got {p1.age}"
assert p1.greet() == "Hi, I'm Alice and I'm 30 years old.", f"greet() returned: {p1.greet()}"

p2 = Person("Bob", 25)
assert p2.greet() == "Hi, I'm Bob and I'm 25 years old.", f"greet() returned: {p2.greet()}"
assert p1.name != p2.name, "Each instance should have its own name"
print("All tests passed!")
hints: Use 'self.name = name' to store an instance attribute.
The greet() method needs 'self' as its first parameter to access instance attributes.
Use an f-string to build the greeting: f"Hi, I'm {self.name} and I'm {self.age} years old."

challenges.title: BankAccount
challenges.description:
Create a class `BankAccount` with:
- `__init__(self, owner, balance=0)` that stores the owner name and starting balance
- `deposit(amount)` that adds the amount to the balance
- `withdraw(amount)` that subtracts the amount from balance IF sufficient funds exist; returns True on success, returns False if insufficient funds (balance should not change on failure)
challenges.test_code:
acct = BankAccount("Alice", 100)
assert acct.owner == "Alice", f"owner should be 'Alice', got '{acct.owner}'"
assert acct.balance == 100, f"balance should be 100, got {acct.balance}"

acct.deposit(50)
assert acct.balance == 150, f"After depositing 50, balance should be 150, got {acct.balance}"

result = acct.withdraw(30)
assert result == True, "withdraw(30) should return True"
assert acct.balance == 120, f"After withdrawing 30, balance should be 120, got {acct.balance}"

result = acct.withdraw(200)
assert result == False, "withdraw(200) should return False (insufficient funds)"
assert acct.balance == 120, f"Balance should remain 120 after failed withdrawal, got {acct.balance}"

default_acct = BankAccount("Bob")
assert default_acct.balance == 0, "Default balance should be 0"
print("All tests passed!")
hints: In withdraw(), check if self.balance >= amount before subtracting.
Remember to return True after a successful withdrawal and False if funds are insufficient.
The deposit method just needs: self.balance += amount
